solution:

So answer is $\frac{40}{c} \cos \omega\left(t-\frac{z}{c}\right) \hat{j}$

solution:

The relation between the magnitudes of electric field, magnetic field and the speed of light is given by

EB=C

⇒ E=B×C
⇒ E=5×10-8×3×108=15 V m-1

We know that the direction of propagation of electromagnetic wave is along E→×B→.

So, E→=15 i^ V m-1

Magnetic force of a moving charge is given by FB=qvB0

Using relation between magnetic and electric field, we have

FEFB=cv=3×1083×107=10:1

subject: Physics
solution:
Theoretical
A-II, B-III, C-IV, D-I

A. $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt} \oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{a}$ → Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction (II)
B. $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 \left(I + \epsilon_0 \frac{d\Phi_E}{dt}\right)$ → Ampere-Maxwell law (III)
C. $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{a} = \frac{1}{\epsilon_0} \int \rho \, dv$ → Gauss's law of electrostatics (IV)
D. $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I$ → Ampere's circuital law (I)

subject: Physics
solution:
The speed of electromagnetic wave in free space is given by

$\mathrm{C}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \epsilon_{0}}}$

In medium, $\mathbf{v}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{k} \in_{0} \mu_{0}}} \ldots$ (ii)

Dividing equation (i) by(ii), we get $\therefore \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{V}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{n}$

$\frac{1}{2} \epsilon_{0} \mathrm{E}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{C}=$ intensity $=\frac{1}{2} \epsilon_{0} \mathrm{kE}^{2} \mathrm{v}$

$\therefore \mathrm{E}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{kE}^{2} \mathrm{v}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{E_{0}^{2}}{E^{2}}=\frac{k V}{C}=\frac{n^{2}}{n} \Rightarrow \frac{E_{0}}{E}=\sqrt{n}$

similarly

$\frac{\mathrm{B}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{C}}{2 \mu_{0}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} \mathrm{v}}{2 \mu_{0}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{B}_{0}}{\mathrm{~B}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{n}}}$

solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{m}} \times \mu_{\mathrm{m}}}{\varepsilon_0 \times \mu_0}=\frac{\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}^2}}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}^2}} \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \times \mu_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{c}^2}{\mathrm{v}^2} \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \times 2=\frac{\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2}{\left(1.5 \times 10^8\right)^2} \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \times 2=4 \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
The maximum electric force acting on the electron is $F_e = eE_0$.

The maximum magnetic force acting on the electron is $F_m = evB_0$, where $v$ is the speed of the electron and $B_0$ is the maximum magnetic field.

The ratio of the maximum electric force to the maximum magnetic force is given by $\dfrac{F_e}{F_m} = \dfrac{eE_0}{evB_0} = \dfrac{E_0}{vB_0}$.

For an electromagnetic wave, the ratio of the amplitudes of the electric and magnetic fields is $\dfrac{E_0}{B_0} = c$, where $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum.

Substituting this into the ratio of forces yields $\dfrac{F_e}{F_m} = \dfrac{c}{v}$.

Given $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and $v = 1.5 \times 10^6$ m/s, we get $\dfrac{F_e}{F_m} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 200$.

Answer: $200$

Electromagnetic wave carries energy shared equally in magnetic and electric field which is given by, =12ε0E2=B22μ0, as E=cB and c2=1μ0ε0.

Also, Electromagnetic wave carries momentum and therefore exerts force when it strikes a surface.

subject: Physics
solution:
Power of the source, $P = 15 \text{ kW} = 15 \times 10^3 \text{ J/s}$

Number of photons emitted per second, $n = 2.5 \times 10^{22} \text{ s}^{-1}$

Energy of one photon, $E = \dfrac{P}{n} = \dfrac{15 \times 10^3}{2.5 \times 10^{22}} = 6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Using $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$, we get $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 330 \text{ nm}$

The wavelength $330 \text{ nm}$ lies in the ultraviolet region of the electromagnetic spectrum.

Answer: Ultraviolet

subject: Physics
solution: The bombarding electrons can eject electrons from the inner shells of the atoms of the metal target. Those vacancies will be quickly filled by electrons dropping from higher levels, emitting x-rays with sharply defined frequencies associated with the difference between the atomic energy levels of the target atoms. Hence, X-rays are emitted when a metal target is bombarded with high energy electrons.

subject: Physics
solution:
The intensity of an electromagnetic wave is
$I = \frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 c E_0^2$.
From the relation $E_0 = c B_0$, we get $I = \frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 c^3 B_0^2$.
Solving for magnetic field amplitude:
$B_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2I}{\epsilon_0 c^3}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{14}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times (3 \times 10^8)^3}} = \sqrt{\frac{8.0 \times 10^{14}}{2.39 \times 10^{14}}}$
$= \sqrt{3.347} = 1.83$ T.

subject: Physics
solution:
Speed of electromagnetic wave in vacuum: $c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}$
Speed in a medium with permeability $\mu = 2\mu_0$ and dielectric constant $k = 3$:
$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \epsilon}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\mu_0 \times 3\epsilon_0}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6\mu_0\epsilon_0}}$
$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\epsilon_0}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{6}}$
Ratio of speeds: $\frac{c}{v} = \sqrt{6}$
Therefore: $c : v = \sqrt{6} : 1$
